macro_segments.title: Three Destructive Angels and Doeg the Edomite's Downfall

micro_segments.title: Three destructive angels that befell Doeg: forgetting Torah, burning of soul, scattering of ashes
Doeg and Achitofel never met; they lived in different eras during Saul and David respectively
Both Doeg and Achitofel did not live past half their lives; teaching about those with blood on their hands

macro_segments.title: David's Relationship with Achitofel: From Rabbi to Rival

micro_segments.title: Achitofel's three titles in David's life: rabbi, friend, and student, traced through Biblical verses
The heel that Achitofel raised against David; trust betrayed through close counsel

macro_segments.title: David's Self-Imposed Trial and the Meaning of Temptation (Nisayon)

micro_segments.title: A person should never bring himself to be tested; David asked for trial and failed
Etymology of nisayon from nes (flag/banner); trials meant to show success, not failure
David's complaint: Why are the patriarchs mentioned in prayer but not him? His request to be tested
God agrees to test David in sexual matters and warns him in advance

macro_segments.title: The Incident with Bathsheba: Narrative, Complications, and Rabbinic Interpretation

micro_segments.title: David rises at evening and switches his bedtime; question about the unusual timing and circumstances
The forgotten halakha: the small limb (sexual drive) that when satiated is hungry, when starved is satisfied
David walks on the roof, sees a woman bathing; Satan appears as a bird; David shoots an arrow
David inquires about the woman; she is identified as Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam, wife of Uriah
Bathsheba's grandfather is Achitofel; the lineage connects to earlier treachery
David sends messengers, Bathsheba comes to him, he sleeps with her; she ritually purifies and returns

macro_segments.title: David's Spiritual Response and Psalmic Confession of Sin

micro_segments.title: Psalm 139: David speaks of being tested by fire; wishes his mouth had been muzzled
The bird imagery in Psalm 11; David asks forgiveness so enemies don't say a bird overcame him
Psalm 51: 'To You alone have I sinned'; understanding David's transgression as against God only
Rava's interpretation: David could have overcome his inclination but didn't, to avoid appearing greater than God
David's limp and pain: Bathsheba was destined for David from creation but came to him through affliction
Alternative teaching: Bathsheba was indeed for David but he took her too early

macro_segments.title: David's Eighteen Wives and the Purpose of His Suffering

micro_segments.title: David had eighteen wives and fulfilled his marital obligations even while ill
How could David need another woman when he already had eighteen wives? The point of his test

macro_segments.title: David's Attempt at Idolatry and God's Negotiations Over Forgiveness

micro_segments.title: David sought to engage in idolatry; Chushai the Archite dissuaded him, explaining a king must not do this
David reasons: better to be suspected of idolatry than to have a son who will kill him
God cannot fully erase David's sin; even the tiny yud taken from Sarah screams for years
Solomon will quote in Proverbs the consequences of taking another man's wife; not all is forgiven in David's lifetime

macro_segments.title: Leprosy and Six Months: The Duration and Cost of David's Affliction

micro_segments.title: David was a metzora (leper) for six months; the Shechina departed from him and the Sanhedrin withdrew
The six months are derived from the discrepancy in David's reign: 40 years total but 33 years 6 months in Jerusalem

macro_segments.title: David's Attempt at Idolatry and God's Negotiations Over Forgiveness (continued)

micro_segments.title: God tells David: you will not know of your forgiveness in your lifetime, but Solomon will know in his

macro_segments.title: The Building of the Temple and the Sign of David's Forgiveness

micro_segments.title: When Solomon built the Temple and tried to bring the ark into the Holy of Holies, the gates clung together
Solomon sang twenty-four songs to open the gates; they would not open
Solomon invokes Psalm 24 about lifting heads and the King of Glory entering
The angels thought Solomon meant himself; Solomon clarifies he means God, the true King of Glory
Only when Solomon mentions his father David's merits does God answer and the gates open
At that moment, all Israel knew that God had forgiven David for that sin

macro_segments.title: Gechazi's Transgression: Taking Payment for God's Healing and Causing Others to Sin

micro_segments.title: Elisha traveled to Damascus to bring Gechazi back in repentance, but Gechazi refused to return
Gechazi's reasoning: one who sins and causes many to sin has no capacity for repentance
The metaphor of angels: one who causes others to sin carries their bad angels and cannot shed them

macro_segments.title: Naaman's Healing and Gechazi's Theft of Gifts

micro_segments.title: Naaman, commander of the Aramean army, comes to Elisha with leprosy seeking to be healed
Elisha directs Naaman to dip in the Jordan; Naaman is healed but questions why the Jordan is special
Naaman insists on giving gifts to Elisha; Elisha refuses, saying healing came from God's power
Gechazi leaves Elisha and takes all the gifts that Naaman offered, placing them in storage
Elisha has a vision and sees Gechazi receiving the rewards of eight despicable creatures

macro_segments.title: Gechazi's Curse: Leprosy for Him and His Descendants

micro_segments.title: Elisha pronounces: Naaman's leprosy will cleave to Gechazi and his descendants forever
Gechazi departs white with leprosy; he is identified as one of four lepers at the gate
According to Rabbi Yochanan, Gechazi and his three sons are the four lepers at the gate

topical_tags.term: Not living past half one's life
To You alone have I sinned
